diagrams.title: General Tree Structure with All Terminology Labels
diagrams.description: A complete labeled tree diagram showing all basic tree terminology in a single hierarchical structure
elements: Root node (A) at the top with label 'ROOT'
Level 0: Node A
Level 1: Nodes B and C (children of A, siblings to each other)
Level 2: Nodes D, E (children of B), Node F (child of C)
Level 3: Node G (child of D)
Parent-child relationships shown with downward arrows
Leaf nodes: E, F, G (nodes with no children)
Branches: Lines connecting parent to children
Path: A-B-D-G highlighted
Ancestors of G: A, B, D
Descendants of B: D, E, G
Subtree rooted at B: B, D, E, G
diagrams.purpose: Students learn to identify and label all basic tree components (root, parent, child, leaf, sibling, ancestor, descendant) in a single comprehensive diagram

diagrams.title: Tree Levels, Depth, and Height Visualization
diagrams.description: Side-by-side comparison showing how level, depth, and height are calculated
elements: Same tree structure used for all measurements
Level numbers shown in blue circles beside each node (Level 0 at root)
Depth labels in green for each node (root depth = 0)
Height labels in red for each subtree and entire tree
Height of leaf node (E, F, G) = 0
Height of node D = 1 (to G)
Height of node B = 2 (to G)
Height of node C = 1 (to F)
Height of root A = 3 (to deepest leaf G)
Arrow annotations showing measurement direction
diagrams.purpose: Clarifies the often-confused concepts of level, depth, and height with explicit calculations for each node

diagrams.title: Tree Degree Illustration
diagrams.description: Diagram showing nodes with different degrees to illustrate the degree concept
elements: Node A with degree 2 (has 2 children B, C)
Node B with degree 2 (has 2 children D, E)
Node C with degree 1 (has 1 child F)
Node D with degree 1 (has 1 child G)
Nodes E, F, G with degree 0 (leaf nodes)
Degree values displayed inside each node
Maximum degree of tree = 2 (shown as 2| in legend)
Binary tree definition: Maximum degree = 2
diagrams.purpose: Helps students understand degree as the count of children and identifies binary trees as having maximum degree of 2

diagrams.title: Binary Tree vs General Tree Comparison
diagrams.description: Two separate tree diagrams comparing binary tree rules versus general tree rules
elements: Binary Tree (left): Each node has at most 2 children, left and right pointers shown, null children represented as empty squares
General Tree (right): Nodes can have any number of children, represented with varying child counts
Both trees use same node labels (A,B,C,D) for comparison
Dotted circles show positions where binary tree has null slots
Arrows indicate flexible child positions in general tree
diagrams.purpose: Demonstrates the key difference between binary trees (max 2 children per node) and general trees (unlimited children), establishing the foundation for binary tree study

charts.title: Tree Terminology Quick Reference
charts.content:
| Term | Definition | Example (from sample tree) |
|------|------------|---------------------------|
| Node | Basic element containing data | A, B, C, D, E, F, G |
| Root | Topmost node with no parent | A |
| Parent | Node with one or more children | A is parent of B, C |
| Child | Node with a parent | B, C are children of A |
| Leaf | Node with no children | E, F, G |
| Sibling | Nodes sharing same parent | B and C are siblings |
| Ancestor | Node on path from root to node | A, B, D are ancestors of G |
| Descendant | Nodes in subtree below a node | D, E, G are descendants of B |
| Degree | Number of children | B has degree 2 |
| Level | Distance from root (root = 0) | C is at level 1 |
| Depth | Number of edges from root to node | G has depth 3 |
| Height | Longest path from node to leaf | Height of A = 3 |
| Path | Sequence of edges from one node to another | A-B-D-G |
| Subtree | Tree rooted at a node and its descendants | Subtree at B includes B,D,E,G |

charts.title: Tree Classification Flowchart
charts.content:
START: Tree Data Structure
 |
 v
Is each node limited to maximum 2 children?
 |
 |--YES--> Binary Tree
 | |
 | v
 | Is it a binary search tree (ordered)?
 | |
 | |--YES--> BST (Search operations O(log n))
 | |
 | |--NO--> Plain Binary Tree
 | |
 | v
 | Is it balanced?
 | |
 | |--YES--> AVL Tree / Red-Black Tree
 | |
 | |--NO--> Unbalanced Binary Tree
 |
 |--NO--> General Tree (n-ary tree)
 |
 v
 How many children maximum?
 |
 |--2--> 2-ary Tree
 |
 |--n--> N-ary Tree (B-trees used in databases)
 |
 |--Limited--> B-Tree / B+Tree (for disk access)

charts.title: Tree vs Linear Data Structures
charts.content:
| Characteristic | Array | Linked List | Stack/Queue | Tree |
|----------------|-------|-------------|-------------|------|
| Structure | Linear | Linear | Linear | Hierarchical |
| Storage | Contiguous | Scattered | Scattered | Scattered |
| Access Time | O(1) | O(n) | O(n) | O(log n) to O(n) |
| Insertion | O(n) | O(1) | O(1) | O(log n) |
| Deletion | O(n) | O(1) | O(1) | O(log n) |
| Search | O(log n) sorted | O(n) | O(n) | O(log n) balanced |
| Traversal | Sequential | Sequential | FIFO/LIFO | Pre/In/Post-order |
| Memory | Low | Medium | Medium | Medium |
| Use Case | Fast access | Dynamic size | Function calls | Hierarchical data |

branches:
Basic Components
 - Node (contains data + pointers)
 - Edge (connection between nodes)
 - Root (topmost node)
 - Path (node sequence)

Node Relationships
 - Parent (has children)
 - Child (has parent)
 - Sibling (same parent)
 - Ancestor (above in path)
 - Descendant (below in subtree)

Tree Properties
 - Degree (children count)
 - Level (root = 0)
 - Height (longest path down)
 - Depth (edges from root)
 - Leaf nodes (degree 0)

Types of Trees
 - Binary Tree (max 2 children)
 - Binary Search Tree
 - AVL Tree (self-balancing)
 - B-Tree (for databases)
 - Expression Tree

Operations
 - Traversal (Pre/In/Post-order)
 - Insertion
 - Deletion
 - Searching
 - Finding min/max

Applications
 - File systems
 - Database indexing
 - Compilers (parse trees)
 - DOM structure
 - Decision trees
mindMaps.purpose: Provides holistic overview connecting all tree concepts, showing how basic components relate to types, operations, and real-world applications for better retention and context
